content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
April 4, 1997
The Honorable Carl Washington
Assembly Member, 5211 District
State Capitol
Post Office Box 942849
Sacramento, California 94249-000 1
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-119
Dear Assembly Member Washington:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions ofthe
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
QUESTIONS
1. As an ordained minister, may you accept payments for continuing your ministry and
conducting church services now that you are an Assembly Member?
2. What are the limitations on your acceptance of gifts for purposes of the gift limits as
they relate to your position as an elected official and an ordained minister?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Payments you receive for continuing your ministry and conducting church services are
income received in the practice of a bona fide business, trade, or profession, and therefore are not
prohibited honoraria.
2. Gifts given to you by members of your congregation are normally subject to the limit
on gifts which may be accepted by a member of the Legislature. However, presents exchanged
between you and a member of your congregation on holidays, birthdays, or similar occasions are
not subject to the gift limit or reportable when presents exchanged are not substantially
disproportionate in value.
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-97-119
Page 2
FACTS
You are a member of the California State Assembly. Prior to your position as an
Assembly Member, you were a legislative staffperson who was also employed as an ordained
minister. You wish to continue conducting your ministry and church services. You are
requesting information regarding your responsibilities under the gift and honoraria provisions of
the Act.
ANALYSIS
Honoraria
Under Section 89502(a), no elected state officer shall accept any honorarium. An
honorarium is defined as a payment for any speech given, article published, or attendance at any
public or private conference, convention, or meeting; however, an honorarium does not include
payments earned for personal services provided in connection with an individual’s bona fide
business, trade, or profession. (Regulations 18931 and 18932.)
In the Boatwright Advice Letter, No. A-93-323, this Commission previously advised that
an ordained minister hired as a legislative staffmember may continue to receive payments for
conducting church services and continuing his ministry because those services constitute the
practice of, or employment in, a bona fide business, trade, or profession under the Act.
Accordingly, the payments you receive for conducting church activities and speaking as an
ordained minister at youth camps, retreats, weddings, and funerals would not be prohibited
honoraria.
2 Such payments would, however, constitute income and as such must be reported on
your annual statement of economic interests. (MetcalfAdvice Letter, No. A-95-3 16.)
Gift Limits
Under Section 89503(a), an elected state officer is subject to a limit on the amount of
gifts that may be accepted in any calendar year. That limit is adjusted biennially to account for
inflation and is currently set at $290. (Section 89503.) No member of the Legislature may
accept a gift from any single source if the total value of gifts from that source will exceed $290
in a calendar year. (MetcalfAdvice Letter, supra.)
2 In the Boatwright Letter, supra, we cautioned that the “bona fide business” exception
does not apply to a business whose sole or predominant activity is making speeches. Though
your facts do not indicate this is an issue in your case, we extend the same cautionary note here.
Additionally, please note that the exception contained herein is limited to speaking engagements
within the scope of your duties as a minister and does not apply to speeches unconnected to that
activity.


File No. A-97-119
Page 3
Section 89503(e), provides that gifts exchanged between individuals on holidays,
birthdays, or other similar occasions are not prohibited or limited as long as the gifts exchanged
are not substantially disproportionate in value. Further, Regulation 1 8942(a)(8) provides that
gifts exchanged between an official and any other individual, except a lobbyist, on holidays,
birthdays, or similar occasions, which are not substantially disproportionate in value, need not be
reported as gifts on the official’s statement of economic interests.
Accordingly, if you and members of your congregation exchange gifts on holidays,
birthdays, or similar occasions, and if the gifts exchanged are not substantially disproportionate
in value, such gifts need not be reported on your annual statement of economic interests.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel A9%
By:
Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
SGC:LD:ak
